Galaxies” and would like to thank Uli Klein
for continuous support and Neb Duric for
fruitful discussions. FW appreciates the help
of the staff of the Array Operation Center of
the NRAO during his stay in Socorro, New
Mexico and also wants to thank the staff of
the Calar Alto observatory in Spain for excellent working conditions. FW is grateful
to Jan Palouš for illuminating discussions regarding the numerical simulations of holes
and shells during his stays at the Academy
of Science in Prague, Czech Republic. EB acknowledges a grant awarded by CONACyT
(grant number 0460P–E) and is grateful for
the support by the Graduiertenkolleg without which this collaborative effort would not
have been possible. Last but not least we
would like to thank Klaas de Boer for carefully reading the manuscript and an anonymous referee for useful suggestions. This research has made use of the NASA/IPAC Extragalactic Database (NED) which is operated by the Jet Propulsion Laboratory, Caltech, under contract with the National Aeronautics and Space Administration (NASA),
NASA’s Astrophysical Data System Abstract
Service (ADS), and NASA’s SkyView.

of IC 2574 obtained with the Calar Alto 2.2–

fully subtracted by removing the continuum
were blanked.

was detected. The velocity of each channel
in km s−1 is plotted in the right hand corner. Greyscale range from 0 (white) to 6
mJy beam−1 (black). Note that the beamsize
(6.4′′ × 5.9′′ as indicated in the box of the top
left hand panel) is smaller than the resolution
of this plot. Coordinates are with respect to
B1950.0 equinox.
Fig. 4.— Global H I profile for IC 2574
(squares) obtained by integrating the channel
maps over the area of the source after subtraction of the continuum background. The
data have been flux corrected (see text) and
are corrected for primary beam attenuation.
The Flux F is given in Jy. The channel spacing is 2.58 km s−1 . For comparison, the profiles published by Rots (1980) (full line) and
MCR94 (dotted line) are also plotted. The
arrow indicates the velocity range affected by
contamination due to galactic emission.

km s−1 ). The beamsize (6.4′′ × 5.9′′ ) is indicated in the lower left.

the 30′′ convolved cube. The velocities are

given in km s−1 . The greyscale image is a linear representation of the H I surface brightness map at full resolution. A circular 30′′
beam is plotted in the lower left hand corner.

show how we defined groups of H II regions
within IC 2574 (with sequence numbers).
Fig. 13.— Enlargement of region B from
Fig. 10. The circles indicate where we detected the H I holes (for identification of the
H I holes, please refer to Fig. 10). The boxes
show how we defined groups of H II regions
within IC 2574 (with sequence numbers).

Fig. 7.— Velocity dispersion (second moment) map of IC 2574. The greyscale is a
linear representation of the velocity dispersion ranging between 4 km s−1 (white) and 13
km s−1 (black). The beamsize (6.4′′ × 5.9′′ ) is
indicated in the lower left.

Fig. 14.— Enlargement of region C from
Fig. 10. The circles indicate where we detected the H I holes (for identification of the
H I holes, please refer to Fig. 10). The boxes
show how we defined groups of H II regions
within IC 2574 (with sequence numbers).

Fig. 8.— Typical examples of position velocity diagrams. a) pV diagram of a Type 3 hole
(hole number 21). b) rV diagram of the same
hole. c) pV diagram of a Type 1 hole (number 30) and d) pV diagram of a Type 2 hole
(number 33).

Fig. 15.— Comparison of the relative distribution, in percentage, of the indicative masses
of the H I holes in Ho II, IC 2574, M 33 and
M 31.

Fig. 9.— Location of the H I holes in IC 2574
(with numbers). The greyscale map is a linear
representation of the H I surface brightness
map. For the largest holes (13, 23, and 26),
see text.

Fig. 16.— Comparison of the relative distribution, in percentage, of the energies required
to produce the H I holes in Ho II, IC 2574,
M 33 and M 31.

Fig. 10.— Hα image of IC 2574 overlaid with
the the discovered holes in its neutral interstellar medium (with numbers). Regions A–C
are enlarged in Figs. 12–14, respectively. Note
that almost all Hα emission in IC 2574 is located on the rims of H I shells (see text).

Fig. 17.— Comparison of the relative distribution, in percentage, of the diameters of the
H I holes in Ho II, IC 2574, M 33 and M 31.
Fig. 18.— Comparison of the relative distribution, in percentage, of the ages of the H I
holes in Ho II, IC 2574, M 33 and M 31.

Fig. 11.— Hα image of IC 2574 overlaid with
one contour of the H I surface brightness map
at 1.7 × 1021 atoms cm−2 . Note that regions
where we find Hα emission coincide with high
H I surface brightness.

Fig. 19.— Comparison of the relative distribution, in percentage, of the expansion velocities of the H I holes in Ho II, IC 2574, M 33
and M 31.

Fig. 12.— Enlargement of region A from
Fig. 10. The circles indicate where we detected the H I holes (for identification of the
H I holes, please refer to Fig. 10). The boxes

Fig. 20.— The relation between expansion velocity and diameter in each of the four galaxies. Crosses represent observed values. The
plotted curves are lines of constant energy
29

(based on Chevalier 1974) at levels of 1049 ,
1050 , 1051 and 1052 ergs (from left to right).
The straight lines correspond to constant age
and are drawn at 107 years (steepest slope),
5 × 107 years and 108 years.

new tiny hole (D<60pc), rim hole 38
diffuse HI
on rim of hole 39, diffuse HI

Hα fills hole 32!

new tiny hole (D<60pc)
on rim of hole 22, diffuse HI

on rim of hole 24. diffuse HI
on rim of holes 21,22,24, high ∆v!
on rim of holes 21,22
a lot of HI in region

a lot of HI in region
new tiny hole (D<60pc)

diffuse HI
diffuse HI
Hα same as rim of hole 8!
on rim of holes 8,13, high ∆v
on rim of holes 23,29

hardly any HI, high ∆v
